questions.question_text: A home inspector notes that the house has aluminum wiring throughout. Why is this a concern for buyers?
questions.correct_answer: It creates a higher fire risk at connections
distractors: Aluminum wiring cannot handle any modern appliance loads
It is illegal in all New York municipalities
Insurance companies refuse to cover aluminum wiring
questions.explanation: Aluminum wiring, common in homes built during the 1960s and 1970s, expands and contracts more than copper, creating loose connections that can overheat and cause fires. While not illegal, it requires special connectors and is a material fact that should be disclosed to buyers.

questions.question_text: What does a higher R-value indicate about insulation material?
distractors: Higher fire resistance rating
Greater moisture absorption capacity
Thinner material with equal performance
questions.explanation: R-value measures a material's thermal resistance, or its ability to resist the transfer of heat. The higher the R-value, the better the insulation performs at keeping heat in during winter and out during summer. Building codes specify minimum R-values for different parts of a structure.

questions.question_text: A buyer is purchasing a home with a renovated basement that includes a new bathroom and bedroom. The seller cannot produce a building permit for the work. What risk does this present?
questions.correct_answer: The municipality may require removal of unpermitted work
distractors: The renovation automatically becomes legal after five years
Only commercial renovations require building permits
The buyer's homeowner insurance will cover any issues
questions.explanation: Building permits and a certificate of occupancy are required for significant renovations. Unpermitted work may violate building codes, creating safety hazards. The municipality can require the owner to obtain retroactive permits, bring work up to code, or in some cases remove the work entirely.

questions.question_text: In residential framing, which structural member sits directly on top of the foundation wall and supports the floor joists?
distractors: The ridge board
The header joist
The lally column
questions.explanation: The sill plate, also called the mudsill, is the first piece of wood framing that rests on top of the foundation wall. It is bolted to the foundation and provides the base on which floor joists and the rest of the framing structure are built. Lally columns are steel posts that support girders in the basement.

questions.question_text: A property has a 100-amp electrical panel with fuses rather than circuit breakers. An agent is preparing a listing description. Which statement about this system is accurate?
questions.correct_answer: Fuse panels are outdated and may deter lenders
distractors: Fuse panels are illegal under current building codes
100-amp service exceeds modern household needs
Fuses are safer than circuit breakers overall
questions.explanation: While fuse panels are not illegal if properly maintained, they are considered outdated. Many lenders and insurers view them unfavorably because they lack the automatic reset feature of circuit breakers. Modern homes typically require 200-amp service, so 100-amp fuse panels may also be inadequate for today's electrical demands.

questions.question_text: Which homeowner's insurance policy form is most commonly used for single-family residences?
distractors: HO-1 basic form
HO-4 tenant's form
HO-6 condominium form
questions.explanation: The HO-3 special form is the most widely used policy for owner-occupied single-family homes. It provides open-peril coverage on the dwelling (covering all risks except those specifically excluded) and named-peril coverage on personal property, offering broader protection than HO-1 or HO-2 forms.

questions.question_text: A buyer is purchasing a co-op apartment in Manhattan. Which insurance policy form covers the unit owner's personal property and liability?
distractors: HO-3 special form
HO-6 condominium form
HO-8 market value form
questions.explanation: Co-op shareholders do not own real property; they own shares in a corporation and hold a proprietary lease. Because of this ownership structure, co-op residents use the HO-4 tenant's form, which covers personal property, liability, and additional living expenses but not the building structure itself.

questions.question_text: A homeowner in a FEMA-designated flood zone applies for insurance but is denied by three private carriers. Through which program can they obtain property coverage in New York?
questions.correct_answer: NYPIUA, the FAIR plan for high-risk properties
distractors: The National Flood Insurance Program only
The state Department of Financial Services directly
Standard HO-3 policies cannot exclude flood zones
questions.explanation: The New York Property Insurance Underwriting Association (NYPIUA), known as the FAIR plan, provides basic property insurance to owners who cannot obtain coverage in the voluntary market. For flood coverage specifically, the National Flood Insurance Program is required in FEMA flood zones, but NYPIUA addresses the broader property insurance need.

questions.question_text: A property is located in a FEMA-designated Special Flood Hazard Area and the buyer is obtaining a federally backed mortgage. What insurance is required?
distractors: Standard HO-3 coverage with a flood endorsement
Only NYPIUA coverage is required
No special insurance if the property has never flooded
questions.explanation: Federal law requires that properties in FEMA-designated Special Flood Hazard Areas with federally backed mortgages carry flood insurance through the National Flood Insurance Program (NFIP). Standard homeowner policies exclude flood damage, so a separate NFIP policy or equivalent private flood insurance must be obtained.

questions.question_text: An appraiser values a 120-year-old Victorian home at $600,000 market value, but the replacement cost to rebuild with modern materials is $900,000. Which policy form is designed for this situation?
questions.correct_answer: HO-8, which insures at market value
distractors: HO-3, which covers full replacement cost
HO-5, which provides comprehensive coverage
HO-2, which uses broad named-peril coverage
questions.explanation: HO-8 is designed for older homes where replacement cost significantly exceeds market value. It insures the dwelling at market value rather than replacement cost, allowing owners to obtain affordable coverage. This policy uses functional replacement, meaning repairs may use modern equivalents rather than matching original materials.
